coredata.dc:title: Carbonaceous 0D/2D composite photocatalyst for degradation of organic dyes
coredata.dc:creator: Cheng, Liqun
Dong, Xiaoping
coredata.dc:description: Photocatalysis technology has stimulated extensive research interest in various fields, but several limitations, such as the inefficient separation ratio of photo-generated charges and the possible release of metal ions from metal oxides or sulfides, need to be overcome before its practical application. Herein, we fabricated a metal-free carbonaceous composite photocatalyst via modification of 2D polymeric carbon nitride (PCN) with 0D graphene quantum dots (GQDs) by an ultrasound-assisted route. Characterization results clearly indicated the formation of 0D/2D composite structure with an intimately contacted interface, and the type II band structure promotes the charge separation between PCN and GQDs. As a result, an enhanced photocatalytic activity for degradation of dyes was achieved by the GQDs/PCN in comparison to the single PCN and GQDs. Furthermore, multiple cycling tests reflected the GQDs/PCN had relatively high photocatalytic durability. This study provides a facile method to construct carbonaceous composite and demonstrates it is a promising photocatalyst for environmental remediation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphitic carbon nitride
Graphene quantum dots
Composite
Photocatalysis
Dye degradation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Physicochemical properties of GQDs/PCN composite photocatalyst
Photocatalytic performance of GQDs/PCN composite photocatalyst

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Zhejiang Province

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Digital photograph and (b) UV–vis absorption spectrum of GQDs solution; (c) TEM image of GQDs; (d) SEM image of PCN; (e) Digital photograph of GQDs/PCN suspension; (f) SEM image, (g) schematic illustration, (h) TEM image and HRTEM image of GQDs/PCN sample.
(a) XRD patterns, (b) FTIR spectra and (c) DRS of various samples.

Photocatalytic performance of GQDs/PCN for degradation of RhB under visible light irradiation: (a) optimization of GQDs content; (b) UV–vis spectral change of RhB solution by the optimized GQDs/PCN catalyst. (c) photocatalytic RhB degradation efficiency, (d) pseudo-first-order kinetics fitting and (e) the apparent rate constants of GQDs, PCN and GQDs/PCN samples; (f) photocatalytic durability of the optimized GQDs/PCN sample.
(a) EIS Nyquist plots and (b) photocurrent response of GQDs, PCN and GQDs/PCN samples.
(a) Tauc plot and (b) Mott-Schottky curve of PCN; (c) Mott-Schottky curve of GQDs; (d) band alignments of PCN and GQDs; (e) Possible photocatalytic mechanism of GQDs/PCN for removal of RhB.
Influence of adding various quenchers on the photocatalytic activity of 0D/2D GQDs/PCN composite photocatalyst.

head.ce:title: Carbonaceous 0D/2D composite photocatalyst for degradation of organic dyes

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hangzhou Vocational & Technical College, No. 68, Xueyuan Street, Xiasha Higher Education Zone, Hangzhou, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:address-line: No. 68, Xueyuan Street, Xiasha Higher Education Zone

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Hangzhou Vocational & Technical College, No. 68, Xueyuan Street, Xiasha Higher Education Zone, Hangzhou, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Zhejiang Sci-Tech University, 928 Second Avenue, Xiasha Higher Education Zone, Hangzhou, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:address-line: 928 Second Avenue, Xiasha Higher Education Zone

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Zhejiang Sci-Tech University, 928 Second Avenue, Xiasha Higher Education Zone, Hangzhou, P. R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Photocatalysis technology has stimulated extensive research interest in various fields, but several limitations, such as the inefficient separation ratio of photo-generated charges and the possible release of metal ions from metal oxides or sulfides, need to be overcome before its practical application. Herein, we fabricated a metal-free carbonaceous composite photocatalyst via modification of 2D polymeric carbon nitride (PCN) with 0D graphene quantum dots (GQDs) by an ultrasound-assisted route. Characterization results clearly indicated the formation of 0D/2D composite structure with an intimately contacted interface, and the type II band structure promotes the charge separation between PCN and GQDs. As a result, an enhanced photocatalytic activity for degradation of dyes was achieved by the GQDs/PCN in comparison to the single PCN and GQDs. Furthermore, multiple cycling tests reflected the GQDs/PCN had relatively high photocatalytic durability. This study provides a facile method to construct carbonaceous composite and demonstrates it is a promising photocatalyst for environmental remediation.

ce:section.ce:para: Photocatalysis technology has been regarded as one of the most promising strategies for environmental remediation due to its simple reaction equipment, environmentally friendly process, strong redox capacity, and so on [1,2]. Up to date, a large number of photocatalysts have been developed, and exhibited efficient activity for removal of different pollutants, e.g. organic dyes [3–6], phenol compounds [7–9], antibiotics [10–13]. However, the insufficient utilization of natural sunlight and the low quantum efficiency from the low separation rate of photo-generated electron-hole pairs drastically limit the practical application of photocatalysis. What is more, it cannot be neglected that the photo-corrosion of metal oxides or sulfides will discharge transition metal ions and result in the secondary pollution [14,15]. Consequently, it is highly desirable to develop novel metal-free photocatalysts for environmental applications.

In the last decade, polymeric carbon nitride (PCN), a carbonaceous material, has received ever-growing interests due to its low-cost precursors, facile preparation, high stability, non-toxicity and narrow bandgap for visible light absorption [16–19]. Unfortunately, the activity of raw PCN is still unsatisfied because of the high recombination rate of photo-excited carriers. Similar to TiO2, numerous strategies have been focused on increasing the activity of PCN, such as diminishing the dimension to nanosize [20–24], metal or nonmetal element doping [25–28] and coupling with heterogeneous semiconductors [29–32]. Among them, formation of CN based heterojunctions is one of the most effective routes to suppress the recombination of photo-induced charges. Nevertheless, the most used heterogeneous semiconductors are still metal oxides, sulfides or oxometallates. Therefore, the risk of metal ion release is still inevitable.

Graphene is widely used in photocatalysis as an electron acceptor to promote the separation of photo-excited electrons and holes in semiconductor photocatalyst [33]. But, its zero band gap cannot absorb photon to produce photo-generated electron-hole pair. Furthermore, the black graphene covered on the photocatalyst surface will obstruct the photo-absorption of photocatalyst. Diminishing the size of graphene to quantum dimension, the obtained zero-dimensional (0D) graphene quantum dot (GQD) materials present a semiconductor-like band structure [34]. Therefore, the excitation of GQDs by irradiation will generate free charge carriers to initiate a variety of redox reactions. However, high efficient photoluminescence (PL) performance is one of the well-known properties for GQDs [35–37], which means the high recombination of photo-produced electron-hole pairs. Hence, considering the intensive light absorption from visible to UV light, in photocatalysis investigation GQDs are generally employed as photosensitizers in photocatalysis, like dye-sensitized photocatalysts [38–41]. For example, V. Krishnan et al. reported a heterojunction photocatalyst of GQDs modified ZnO nanorods via a hydrothermal synthesis, which was used as a sunlight-driven photocatalyst for removal of organic pollutants [42]. GQDs was doped into a p-n junction photocatalyst of BiOI/MnNb2O6 and the obtained photocatalyst showed greatly enhanced visible-light activity for decomposing antibiotics [39].

In this work, we employed an ultrasound method to prepare carbonaceous composite by depositing 0D GQDs on the surface of 2D PCN. The obtained 0D/2D GQDs/PCN photocatalyst was characterized by various technologies. And, the loading amount of GQDs on PCN was optimized by evaluating the photocatalytic activity for degrading the organic dye Rhodamine B (RhB). Furthermore, we compared the photocatalytic performance of the optimal GQDs/PCN with the single GQDs and PCN, and meanwhile estimated the durability of the GQDs/PCN. Finally, a possible mechanism was proposed based on experimental results.

Pyrene (>97%) was obtained from Chengdu Cologne chemical reagent company (China). Triethanolamine (>99%) and rhodamine B (RhB, >95%) were provided by Shanghai Aladdin industrial corporation (China). Melamine (C3H6N6, 99%) was purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co. Ltd. (China). Other chemicals were obtained from Hangzhou Gaojing Chemistry Co. Ltd. (China). All reagents were of analytical grade and used without further purification. Ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ cm) was prepared from a Millipore Milli-Q purification unit.

20 mg 1,3,6-trinitropyrene and 200 μL triethanolamine were dissolved in 10 mL water under violently magnetic stirring for 30 min. Then, the solution was transferred into Teflon-lined autoclave and heated for 4 h at 200 °C. After being cooled down to room temperature, the resultant was filtered using a 0.15 μm microporous membrane to remove insoluble carbon particles. Subsequently, un-reacted small molecules were purified through dialyzing the solution in a dialysis bag (retained molecular weight of 1000 Da) against ultrapure water for 24 h. After that, another dialysis operation in a dialysis bag (retained molecular weight: 3500 Da) for 24 h was performed to clarify impurities with large size.

The pristine PCN was obtained by thermal polycondensation of melamine following a reported procedure in literatures [43–45]. Then, the composite of GQDs/PCN was prepared via an ultrasound-assisted method. In a detail procedure, 0.2 g PCN and 25 μL GQDs solution (40 mg mL−1) was added into 15 mL water and the mixture was ultrasonically treated for 6 h at room temperature. The suspension was centrifuged at 1000 rpm for 10 min, and the collected solids were washed with water for several times and then dried at 40 °C overnight. The obtained sample was marked as GQDs/PCN-0.50%, in which 0.50% is the mass ratio of GQDs and PCN. Similarly, other GQDs/PCN samples with different GQDs loading contents (0.25%, 0.38%, 1.0%, 2.0%) were also prepared.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were collected on a DX-2700 diffractometer (Dandong Haoyuan Instrument Co. Ltd. China) with Cu Kα radiation. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded on a Nicolet Avatar 370 spectrophotometer using the standard KBr disk method. A Hitachi SU-8100 Field emission scanning electron microscope (SEM) with an energy-dispersive X-ray spectrometer (EDS) was used to characterize the sample morphology and composition. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) observation was performed on a JEOL JEM-2100 electron microscope. UV–vis absorption spectra and diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) were recorded on a Shimadzu 2450 UV–vis spectrometer. The electrochemical tests were performed on a CHI660E electrochemical workstation (Chenhua, Shanghai, China).

Photocatalytic tests were evaluated by degradation of RhB in aqueous solution under visible light irradiation using a 300 W xenon lamp (HSXF300, Beijing NBet) with a 420 nm cutoff filter as the light source. In each experiment, 100 mg of catalyst was added to 100 mL RhB solution (10 mg L−1) in a beaker. Before irradiation, the photocatalyst was stirred in dark for 60 min to establish the adsorption-desorption equilibrium. At given time intervals, 3.5 mL of the solution was collected and subsequently centrifuged to remove the particles. The concentration of solution was analyzed by measuring the maximum absorbance at 554 nm for RhB through a Shimadzu UV-2450 spectrophotometer.

To reveal the effect of various active species on the degradation of dyes, the trapping experiment using different quenchers was performed. Disodium ethylenediaminetetraacetate (EDTA-2Na, 6 mM), benzoquinone (BQ, 0.5 mM) and tert-butylalcohol (TBA, 6 mM) were respectively added into the reaction solution as the scavengers for photo-generated holes (h+), superoxide radicals (·O2 −) and hydroxyl radicals (·OH). The method was similar to the former degradation test.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Physicochemical properties of GQDs/PCN composite photocatalyst
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1a shows the digital photograph of GQDs solution that has good dispersion and high stability in aqueous system. The brown GQDs solution presents intensive spectral absorption from UV to visible region in the UV–vis absorption spectrum (Fig. 1b), where the absorption edge locates at ~603 nm, representing a narrow band gap of 2.06 eV. This result indicates this GQDs material could well absorb visible light and should be a promising photocatalyst candidate for utilization of natural sunlight. Fig. 1c displays the TEM image of GQDs that have relative uniform size. The dimension of GQDs was evaluated to be 3–4 nm.

The pristine PCN from polycondensation of melamine has bulky morphology with particle size of several micrometers (see in Fig. 1d). After ultrasound-treating the bulk PCN in the GQDs solution for 6 h, a stable colloidal suspension was obtained (Fig. 1e). In the meantime, the collected GQDs/PCN sample presents a typical 2D morphology with a lateral size of several hundred nanometers from its SEM image (Fig. 1f). The exfoliation of the bulky PCN into its 2D structure can be ascribed to the ultrasound effect, as well as the GQDs intercalation to diminish the interlayer action of CN layers in PCN [46,47]. The hydrothermally prepared GQDs have plentiful oxygen-containing groups, and in the EDS elemental mapping images (Fig. S1) the distribution of O element is in accordance with those of C and N, indicating the successful modification of PCN with GQDs.

Because of the π–π interaction between the conjugated CN skeleton in PCN and the graphite structure of GQDs, GQDs are firmly loaded on the PCN surface, forming a 0D/2D composite photocatalyst (Fig. 1g). The loading of GQDs was directly demonstrated through TEM observation. Nevertheless similar element composition of GQDs and PCN that results in difficultly distinguish GQDs on PCN surface, some dark zones are still found on the PCN surface (Fig. 1h). These dark zones are assigned to GQDs because their size is consistent with that of GQDs in Fig. 1c. This hypothesis is further proved by the HRTEM image. In Fig. 1i, the clear fringes in the dark zone exhibits lattice spacing of ~0.245 nm that are attributed to the (1120) facet of graphene [48,49].

Fig. 2a illustrates XRD patterns of PCN, GQDs and GQDs/PCN samples. The PCN shows characteristic (002) diffraction at ~27°, representing the periodically stacking of lamellar CN skeleton [50,51]. Another obvious peak at ~13° corresponds to the in-planar (100) diffraction [50–52]. The GQDs has a weak and broad (002) diffraction at ~27°, reflecting the few-layer graphite structure of GQDs. After modification with GQDs, the GQDs/PCN depicts similar peaks to the PCN, which implies the GQDs loading did not affect the crystal structure of PCN. Fig. 2b compares the FTIR spectra of PCN and GQDs/PCN. Absorptions in the region of 1200–1600 cm−1 originate from the typical stretching vibration modes of conjugated CN heterocyclic ring, and the peak at 810 cm−1 is related to the characteristic breathing mode of s-triazine unit [51,52]. There is no evident difference between the PCN and the GQDs/PCN samples, suggesting the well preservation of CN framework in the 0D/2D composite. In addition, the absorption bands at 3000–3600 cm−1 are assigned to the NH and OH stretching vibrations. In comparison to the pure PCN, an enhanced absorption of OH vibration is found in the GQDs/PCN sample, which should result from the plentiful hydroxyl groups on the hydrothermally prepared GQDs. All the above results demonstrate that the 0D/2D GQDs/PCN composite photocatalyst has been successfully fabricated. The change of optical property of PCN after modification with GQDs was studied via UV–vis DRS. As illustrated in Fig. 2c, the PCN spectrum shows strong band-to-band absorption from UV to visible light region. The GQDs/PCN displays a similar absorption onset in visible light region, which may be induced by the low loading amount of GQDs on PCN. But, compared to PCN, the absorption in the range of 200–300 nm is significantly enhanced for the GQDs/PCN. The π-π* transition in GQDs should be responsible for the absorption enhancement, which is also a powerful evidence to imply the coupling of GQDs and PCN.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic performance of GQDs/PCN composite photocatalyst
ce:section.ce:para: Photocatalytic activity of GQDs/PCN was estimated through decomposing RhB under visible light illumination (>420 nm). Firstly, we optimized the loading amount of GQDs on PCN, and the result is presented in Fig. 3a. It is clearly observed that the photocatalytic activity of GQDs/PCN gradually increases as enhancing the GQDs content from 0.25% to 0.50%, and then displays a decrease trend after improving the GQDs content to 1.0% and 2.0%. This decay of activity could be attributed to the shielding effect of overloaded GQDs on PCN surface that would affect the PCN photo-absorption. Thus, we chose the GQDs/PCN-0.50% as the optimal composite photocatalyst for further investigation. Fig. 3b exhibits the spectral evolution of RhB solution with presence of the optimal GQDs/PCN-0.50% photocatalyst. After establishing the adsorption-desorption equilibrium, the characteristic peak of RhB at 554 nm rapidly decreased when the xenon lamp was turn on. A blue-shift of this peak was observed with increase of the irradiation time, which is caused by the gradual de-ethylation process [53,54]. More importantly, the simultaneous decrease of absorptions in UV region indicates the happen of ring opening reaction, meaning a deep oxidation of RhB molecule. It also demonstrates that this metal free composite photocatalyst is a potential candidate for removal of organic pollutants.

We also compared the activity of GQDs/PCN with individual PCN and GQDs (Fig. 3c). In spite of the strong photo-harvest in the visible region, the GQDs sample has no activity for degradation of RhB due to the high recombination rate of photo-excited electron and hole pairs. As a well-known visible-light-driven photocatalyst, the PCN present a significant RhB degradation behavior and ~90% dye molecules was decomposed after 120 min irradiation. The photocatalytic activity was further promoted after modification of PCN with GQDs, where 50% dyes were removed in the first 20 min and the complete degradation was achieved after 80 min illumination. For understanding the photocatalytic process of RhB on photocatalyst, the pseudo-first-order kinetics formula was used to fit photocatalytic data [55,56]:
(1)
− ln
C /
C 0
= k × t

In this equation, C 0 and C are the RhB concentrations at the irradiation time of 0 and t min, k is the apparent rate constant. As illustrated in Fig. 3d, a good linearity implies that the RhB degradation on the GQDs/PCN photocatalyst follows the first-order kinetics. The calculated k value of GQDs/PCN (Fig. 3e) is 3.397 × 10−2 min−1 that is 2.4 and 337 times of the PCN (1.405 × 10−2 min−1) and GQDs (1.009 × 10−4 min−1), respectively. Table S1 compares the photocatalytic performance of GQDs/PCN with some reported GQDs-based or PCN-based photocatalysts. Obviously, the activity of GQDs/PCN is higher than most of these reported photocatalysts. The durability of this GQDs/PCN photocatalyst was evaluated by the successive multiple cycles (Fig. 3f). In the 4th run, the complete degradation of RhB was still achieved, implying the extremely high stability and superior reusability. Furthermore, the GQDs/PCN also presents high activity for removal of other dye, like methyl violet (Fig. S2).

In comparison to the single PCN, the enhanced photocatalytic performance of the GQDs/PCN is considered to originate from the GQDs sensitization, and the 0D/2D composite structure facilitating the charge transport between these two phases. To reveal the migration and separation property of photo-produced charge carriers, electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) and photocurrent response were carried out. Fig. 4 (a) shows the EIS Nyquist plots of photocatalysts. The radius of semicircle-like curve in the high frequency zone represents the resistance of charge transfer (R ct) [57–59]. It is obviously found that the GQDs/PCN has a much smaller R ct value than those of the pure PCN and GQDs, suggesting the much higher migration ability of photo-excited charges in the GQDs/PCN. Their photocurrent response is displayed in (b). Agreeing with the EIS results, the GQDs/PCN has much larger current density than the PCN and GQDs, which can be contributed from the sufficient charge transfer between PCN and GQDs in the composite.

To reveal the transfer pathway of photo-induced electrons and holes in the composite photocatalyst, the detailed band alignment was plotted on basis of experimental results. The band gap (E g) value of PCN is calculated from the Kubelka-Munk equation [60,61]:
(2)
αhν = A
E g
− hν
n / 2

In the formula, α is the absorption coefficient, A is a constant, h is Planck's constant, ν is the frequency of the incident light, and n is 4 for an indirect band gap semiconductor (PCN). Therefore, the E g value of PCN is estimated to 2.55 eV from its Tauc plot in Fig. 5a.

Electrochemical Mott-Schottky technology was used to determine the semiconductor type of PCN and GQDs, as well as their flat-band potential that is usually regarded as the edge potential of conduction band (CB). Both the PCN and the GQDs have a positive slope of their Mott-Schottky curves (Fig. 5b and c), indicating they are n-type semiconductors. The flat-band potentials are estimated to be −1.48 V vs. Ag/AgCl for PCN, namely −1.28 V vs. Normal Hydrogen electrode (NHE), and −0.90 V vs. Ag/AgCl (−0.70 V vs NHE) for GQDs. Accordingly, the band structures of PCN and GQDs are illustrated in Fig. 5d. The CB and the valence band (VB) potentials of PCN are −1.28 eV and 1.27 eV, and the GQDs values are −0.70 eV and 1.36 eV.

After coupling these two phases, a type II heterojunction was formed (Fig. 5e). Under illumination, electrons are excited and transfer to the CB of PCN and GQDs, meanwhile leaving holes on their VB. Following the charge transfer pathway in type II heterojunction, electrons on the CB of PCN will diffuse to the CB of GQDs, and holes transport from the VB of GQDs to the VB of PCN. Consequently, photo-generated electrons and holes are effectively separated on GQDs and PCN, respectively. The accumulated free electrons on the CB of GQDs can react with adsorbed oxygen to produce superoxide radical (O2 −) that can further react with water to form hydroxyl radical (OH). On the other hand, holes on the VB of PCN cannot oxidize water to hydroxyl radical because the redox potential of OH/H2O (2.7 V vs. NHE) is much more positive than the VB potential of PCN. These reactive oxygen species (O2 −, OH and photo-excited hole) will oxidize RhB to small molecules.

The role of different ROSs in the photocatalytic process was revealed via the trapping experiments (Fig. 6 ). We employed benzoquinone (BQ, 0.5 mM), tert-butylalcohol (TBA, 6 mM) and disodium ethylenediaminetetraacetate (EDTA-2Na, 6 mM) aqueous solutions to respectively act as scavengers for O2 −, OH and photo-excited hole [44,50,59]. After introducing BQ and TBA, an obvious decline for the degradation activity of the GQDs/PCN was observed. Especially for BQ, only ~20% RhB molecules were removed after 120 min. However, it is curious that the addition of EDTA-2Na promoted the decomposition efficiency rather than suppressed the RhB removal, which can be attributed to the quenching of photo-excited hole for improving the separation of photo-generated charges. These trapping experiments demonstrate that O2 − radical is the main species, OH is the secondary species and photo-excited hole has no effect on RhB degradation, but eliminating photo-excited hole is helpful for improving the photocatalytic performance.

We have successfully synthesized a metal-free carbonaceous 0D/2D GQDs/PCN photocatalyst through the ultrasound treatment method. The conjugated structure in the layered CN framework of PCN facilitated the loading of GQDs via a π–π interaction, which resulted in a tightly closed interface between GQDs and PCN. In comparison to PCN, an enhanced separation efficiency of photo-generated charges in the GQDs/PCN photocatalyst was achieved. The loading amount of GQDs on PCN was optimized by evaluating the photocatalytic activity for degradation of RhB. The optimal GQDs/PCN-0.50% photocatalyst exhibited a high photocatalytic rate that is 2.4 and 337 times of the PCN and GQDs, respectively. Moreover, a high stability and photocatalytic durability were also proved via 4 successive runs. This work indicates that this metal-free carbonaceous 0D/2D GQDs/PCN composite is an efficient and safe photocatalyst for environmental purification without risk of secondary pollution from metal ion release.
Liqun Cheng: Investigation, Writing - Original Draft.
Xiaoping Dong: Ideas, Funding acquisition, Writing - Review & Editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported from the Zhejiang Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (LY20B070001).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.diamond.2020.108096.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The role of polarization in photocatalysis

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: F. Chen, H. Huang, L. Guo, Y. Zhang, T. Ma, The role of polarization in photocatalysis, Angew. Chem. Int. Ed. 58(2019) 10061-10073.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent advances in MOF-based photocatalysis: environmental remediation under visible light

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A novel magnetic recyclable photocatalyst based on a core-shell metal-organic framework Fe3O4@MIL-100(Fe) for the decolorization of methylene blue dye

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Enhanced charge separation ability and visible light photocatalytic performance of graphitic carbon nitride by binary S, B co-doping

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Co-Pd/BiVO4: high-performance photocatalysts for the degradation of phenol under visible light irradiation

sb:title.sb:maintitle: TiO2 photocatalyst for removal of volatile organic compounds in gas phase-a review

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Shayegan, C. S. Lee, F. Haghighat, TiO2 photocatalyst for removal of volatile organic compounds in gas phase-A review, Chem. Eng. J. 334(2018) 2408-2439.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Photodegradation of phenol via C3N4-agar hybrid hydrogel 3D photocatalysts with free separation

sb:title.sb:maintitle: All-solid-state Z-scheme CdTe/TiO2 heterostructure photocatalysts with enhanced visible-light photocatalytic degradation of antibiotic waste water

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A novel double Z-scheme photocatalyst Ag3PO4/Bi2S3/Bi2O3 with enhanced visible-light photocatalytic performance for antibiotic degradation
